title: How the body develops food sensitivities from chronic stress
author: Kalesh

 I remember the first time I ate a bowl of oatmeal and my stomach felt like it wa...
htmlContent:
Your body started reacting to food it used to handle fine. Here's why.

I remember the first time I ate a bowl of oatmeal and my stomach felt like it was twisting into a knot. I thought it was just a bad batch of oats. Then it happened again. And again. Pretty soon I was reacting to eggs, then almonds, then things I'd eaten my whole life without a second thought. My doctor ran tests. Everything came back "normal." So what was going on?

Here's the thing. I wasn't developing random allergies. My body was developing food sensitivities because my nervous system was stuck in chronic stress. And yours might be too.

Let me explain how this works, because it's not random. It's actually a predictable survival response that makes perfect sense once you understand the science. Stay with me here.

What your nervous system has to do with digesting a sandwich

You have a vagus nerve that runs from your brainstem all the way down to your gut. It's the main highway for communication between your brain and your digestive system. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, showed that this nerve is directly connected to your ability to feel safe enough to digest food properly.

When your nervous system detects safety, your vagus nerve sends signals that tell your gut to relax, release digestive enzymes, and move food through your system at a normal pace. This is what Porges calls the "ventral vagal" state. It's the state where your body can rest, digest, and repair.

But when your nervous system detects threat - and I mean any kind of threat, from a work deadline to a memory of trauma to the sound of someone raising their voice - your vagus nerve gets suppressed. Your body shifts into sympathetic activation, what most people call fight or flight. And digestion gets put on hold.

Think about it. If you're running from a tiger, you don't need to digest your lunch. Your body knows this. It's been programmed for millions of years. So it shuts down digestion to conserve energy for survival. Blood flow moves away from your gut and toward your muscles and heart. Stomach acid production decreases. Gut motility slows down.

Now here's where it gets specific to food sensitivities. When you eat in this state - and most of us eat in this state multiple times a day without even realizing it - your body can't properly break down the proteins in your food. Partially digested proteins leak through your gut lining into your bloodstream. Your immune system sees these foreign particles and attacks them.

The first time this happens, you might not notice anything. But your immune system has a memory. The next time you eat that same food, it mounts a faster, stronger response. And that's how a food sensitivity is born.

This is exactly what Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, has written about for decades. He talks about how unresolved trauma and chronic stress get stored in the body, and how that storage affects every system including digestion. Your body isn't just reacting to the food. It's reacting to the context in which you're eating it, and the state of your nervous system at that moment.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Bessel van der Kolk walks through exactly how trauma reshapes the body, including the gut. It's not light reading, but it's real.

The gut lining is a border wall that can break down

Your gut lining is only one cell thick. That's it. One layer of cells separating your bloodstream from everything you eat. When your nervous system is regulated, those cells are tightly packed together with proteins called tight junctions. Nothing gets through unless it's been properly broken down.

But cortisol - your main stress hormone - directly damages those tight junctions. When you're chronically stressed, your body pumps out cortisol all day long. And cortisol tells those tight junctions to loosen up. Suddenly, particles that should stay in your gut are getting through. This is what researchers call intestinal permeability. Most people call it leaky gut.

I know the term "leaky gut" gets thrown around a lot by wellness influencers selling expensive supplements. And I'm not here to sell you on a trend. But the research on stress-induced intestinal permeability is solid. It's been replicated in multiple studies. When you stress animals, their gut lining becomes more permeable. When you stress humans, the same thing happens.

Here's what that means for you. Every time you eat while stressed, you're potentially training your immune system to attack the food you're eating. And once your immune system learns to attack a food, it doesn't easily unlearn that response. Wild, right?

This is why people develop food sensitivities seemingly out of nowhere. It's not that the food changed. It's that your gut changed. Your nervous system changed. And your immune system responded accordingly.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana breaks down how to work with your vagus nerve in real time, which is exactly what you need if food sensitivities are showing up in your life.

Why elimination diets can backfire if you ignore the nervous system

I've seen so many people go on strict elimination diets and feel worse, not better. They cut out gluten, dairy, soy, eggs, corn, nightshades, everything. And they still have symptoms. Or they feel better for a few weeks and then crash.

Here's why. If your nervous system is still stuck in chronic stress, it doesn't matter what you eat. Your body will find something to react to. Because the problem isn't the food. The problem is that your gut lining is permeable and your immune system is on high alert. You could eat the cleanest food on the planet and your body would still see it as a threat.

I'm not saying diet changes don't matter. They do. But they're not the whole picture. And the wellness industry has done us a disservice by pretending they are.

Think about it this way. If you're in a constant state of hypervigilance, your immune system is also hypervigilant. It's scanning for threats everywhere. And food proteins are just another thing to flag. You can't out-diet a dysregulated nervous system.

What actually helps is addressing both sides. You work on calming your nervous system, and you give your gut a break by removing the foods that are currently triggering reactions. But you don't stay on that restricted diet forever. You use it as a temporary support while you do the deeper work.

Janina Fisher, who writes about trauma and the body, talks about this in her work. She says that the body needs to feel safe before it can let go of protective responses. Your food sensitivities are a protective response. Your body is trying to protect you from what it perceives as threats. It's not wrong. It's just overreacting because it's been through too much.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure stimulation can help shift your body out of that hypervigilant state, which in turn supports better digestion.

Small shifts that actually change how your body handles food

I'm not going to give you a ten-step protocol. That's not how this works. But I will tell you what I've seen make a real difference for people, including myself.

First, slow down when you eat. I mean really slow down. Put your fork down between bites. Chew until the food is liquid. Notice the taste and texture. This might sound basic, but it's actually a powerful nervous system intervention. When you eat slowly, your vagus nerve gets the signal that it's safe to digest. Your body starts producing stomach acid and enzymes. Your gut moves food through at the right pace.

Second, eat in a calm environment. Don't eat at your desk while answering emails. Don't eat in the car. Don't eat while scrolling through news that makes you angry. Find a quiet place, sit down, and just eat. Your nervous system needs the context of safety to do its job.

Third, work on your baseline nervous system regulation. This is the hard part. If you're in chronic stress all day, no amount of eating slowly at dinner is going to fix it. You need to find ways to signal safety to your body throughout the day. That might be a few minutes of deep breathing. It might be a walk outside. It might be putting your hand on your heart and saying something kind to yourself. It might be using earplugs to reduce noise sensitivity if you're easily overwhelmed.

A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower ambient noise without blocking everything out, which can help your nervous system settle.

Fourth, don't chase perfect. You're not going to eat perfectly. You're not going to regulate your nervous system perfectly. That's not the goal. The goal is to move in the direction of safety, one small step at a time. Your body will respond to that gradual shift.

Fifth, consider working with someone who understands the connection between trauma, nervous system, and digestion. A therapist trained in somatic approaches. A nutritionist who gets polyvagal theory. You don't have to figure this out alone.

Your body isn't broken. It's protecting you.

I want to say that again because I think it matters. Your body isn't broken. It's not betraying you. It's doing exactly what it was designed to do, which is protect you from perceived threats. The problem is that your threat detection system got calibrated too high, and now it's flagging food as dangerous when it's not.

This is not your fault. You didn't ask for the chronic stress that reshaped your nervous system. You didn't choose to develop food sensitivities. And you can't just think your way out of this. The body doesn't respond to logic. It responds to safety.

So the real question isn't "what foods should I avoid?" It's "how do I help my body feel safe enough to stop treating food like a threat?"

I don't have a neat answer to that question. Nobody does. It's a process. It takes time. And it's different for every person. But I can tell you that it's possible. I've seen people go from reacting to almost everything to eating foods again. It happened slowly. It happened in fits and starts. But it happened.

Your body wants to heal. It really does. It's been working so hard to keep you alive, even when that meant creating food sensitivities. It's not against you. It's for you. And when you start treating it with that understanding, things can shift.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did something relax? Did something tighten? That's your nervous system talking. It's been talking all along. You just might not have known how to listen.
